serving.natural_language_portion: A standard Singaporean serving of steamed white rice is approximately one rice bowl, weighing around 200g cooked, which is the typical accompaniment to a hawker-centre meal.

nutrient_source.title: Rice, white, cooked — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for cooked white rice (白饭). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record. The input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95, which is preserved.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is the established value for white rice (cooked, plain) from the University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 72 × (25.19 × 200 / 100) / 100 = 72 × 50.38 / 100 = 36.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 231 mg/100g is borderline elevated for a plain grain; potassium at 327 mg/100g partially offsets this, yielding a Na:K ratio of approximately 0.71.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one bowl (200g) and avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or salted fish paste, as cumulative sodium from accompaniments can easily exceed 600 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index of 72 and glycemic load of 36.3 per standard 200g serving; carbohydrate content of 25.19g/100g drives significant postprandial glucose excursion.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict steamed white rice to no more than half a bowl (100g cooked) per meal, pair it with high-fibre vegetables and lean protein to blunt glycaemic response, and consider substituting with brown rice or mixed-grain rice for lower GI alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 231 mg/100g is elevated for a plain staple; fat at 4.21g/100g is higher than expected for plain steamed rice, suggesting possible oil or enrichment; cholesterol is 0 mg.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor total sodium intake from rice and accompaniments combined, limit portion to one bowl, and prefer preparations without added oil or salt to keep saturated fat and sodium within heart-healthy targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed white rice (白饭, Bai Fan) is the foundational staple grain of Singaporean, Chinese, and broader Southeast Asian cuisine. It is prepared by washing polished long-grain or medium-grain white rice and cooking it with water using steaming or absorption boiling until the grains are soft, fluffy, and slightly sticky. The texture is tender with a mild, neutral flavour that makes it an ideal accompaniment to strongly seasoned dishes such as braised meats, stir-fried vegetables, curries, and soups. Per 100g cooked, this HPB-recorded profile provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, and 25.19g carbohydrate, with 3.7g dietary fibre — notably higher than typical plain white rice, suggesting this may be an enriched or mixed-grain product. Sodium is 231 mg/100g, which is elevated for a plain grain and warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index of white rice is approximately 72 (high), making portion control critical for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Potassium at 327 mg/100g and magnesium at 40 mg/100g provide meaningful mineral contributions. In Singaporean hawker culture, a bowl of white rice (approximately 200g) is the default base for a full meal, paired with dishes from economic rice (cai fan) stalls. It is gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher, making it broadly suitable across dietary requirements, though its high GI and moderate sodium content require mindful portioning for clinical populations.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of white rice?
Is white rice good for diabetics?
What is the GI of white rice?
How much sodium is in steamed rice?
Can I eat rice if I have high blood pressure?
White rice vs brown rice nutrition
How much rice should I eat per meal?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95, which is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean food records. GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. All mandatory multilingual names are present. The elevated fibre (3.7g/100g) and fat (4.21g/100g) values are atypical for plain white rice and are noted as caveats, but the HPB source is trusted as the canonical record.
notes: 1. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this specific rice record as of 2026-04-16. 2. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100g by HPB; this is confirmed zero as rice contains no dietary cholesterol. 3. The dietary fibre value of 3.7g/100g and fat value of 4.21g/100g are notably higher than typical plain steamed white rice (usually ~0.4g fibre and ~0.3g fat per 100g cooked). This may reflect a mixed-grain, enriched, or composite rice product in the HPB database. The HPB source value is preserved as canonical; users should verify if this record represents a specific enriched product. 4. Sodium at 231 mg/100g is higher than expected for unsalted steamed rice, which may indicate added salt or a composite preparation. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 200g serving size: GL = 72 × (25.19 × 2) / 100 = 36.3. 6. Ontology codes: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 and SNOMED CT 226355009 are defensible matches for cooked white rice; FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible match for plain cooked white rice in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
